posts.name: Ilkka |_ |_| mme
posts.cooked: Two years ago I bought a bottle of the 2012 Lamy-Caillat Chassagne Caillerets for 54€. I knew it was limited production and getting praise but that’s it. Just today I received an email offer for the 2017 vintage at 1300€/bottle. I had to check wine-searcher and the cheapest price on the free version was 600€. Does anyone know why the sudden and massive price hike? I know this is Burgundy we are talking about, but still. I hope it’s not a case of anyone dying.

same as Bizot… a good, up-and-coming producer suddenly finds a cult following and international buyers jump on the “next big thing.” In my opinion the wines are good but not close to four-digits good.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Wow, I had no idea. Kierdorfwein is selling it for €594 now. Their prices are usually very good. I bought a 6 pack from them a couple of years ago at €47.

posts.name: Asger L a r s e n
posts.cooked: Had a bottle of Romanee 2018 recently - it was amazing. And worth the 200 euro price tag at restaurant. Market price are crazy - sorry to see these wines become investment items
posts.display_username: Asger L a r s e n

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked: Master William Kelley went ga-ga for these wines and that was that …

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Probably twice a month on WB I read about another small producer that just went crazy like this. Wines were $50-100 a year ago and now suddenly they’re $300 or $500 or $1000. Champagne, Burgundy, Northern Rhone most often.

Maybe it’ll be my turn one of these days to own some wines like that.

posts.name: C Chen
posts.cooked: Damn. Absolutely loved the 2017 Caillerets though.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked:
Probably twice a month on WB I read about another small producer that just went crazy like this. Wines were $50-100 a year ago and now suddenly they’re $300 or $500 or $1000. Champagne, Burgundy, Northern Rhone most often.

Maybe it’ll be my turn one of these days to own some wines like that.

It goes in phases, Napa was the same way years ago, 2003 Scarecrow was $150 a bottle, same with other regions and wines like SQN. I looked online at Des Tours, not even Rayas and the 2000 Vacqueyras is at auction for $250, think I paid $25.

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.cooked:
Probably twice a month on WB I read about another small producer that just went crazy like this. Wines were $50-100 a year ago and now suddenly they’re $300 or $500 or $1000. Champagne, Burgundy, Northern Rhone most often.

Maybe it’ll be my turn one of these days to own some wines like that.

What Northern Rhone producer falls into this category? Allemand went from $150 to $300 over a few years, that’s nowhere close to Bizot, Lamy-Caillat, etc. Can’t think of any others…

posts.name: Carter Y.
posts.cooked:
What Northern Rhone producer falls into this category? Allemand went from $150 to $300 over a few years, that’s nowhere close to Bizot, Lamy-Caillat, etc. Can’t think of any others…

Not quite Bizot/ L-C level, but Juge had a decent sharp price jump. A few others that come to mind are Allemand SS and Gonon VV.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Probably twice a month on WB I read about another small producer that just went crazy like this. Wines were $50-100 a year ago and now suddenly they’re $300 or $500 or $1000. Champagne, Burgundy, Northern Rhone most often.

Maybe it’ll be my turn one of these days to own some wines like that.

What Northern Rhone producer falls into this category? Allemand went from $150 to $300 over a few years, that’s nowhere close to Bizot, Lamy-Caillat, etc. Can’t think of any others…

You’re right, my numbers were just for illustration and off the cuff. I’m just noting an overall phenomenon.

There have always been wines that went up a lot in value, but the speed, extent and frequency of these rises is amazing recently.

posts.name: Ilkka |_ |_| mme
posts.cooked: Thanks everyone for the insight. I do realize that this is not a unique phenomenon but very few whites ever reach a four digit price. A producer like PYCM has gone up in price a lot in recent years but even his premier crus are affordable in comparison. Would have loved to hear William Kelley’s take but I suppose it is indeed simply a case of a small production finding a strong following.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Would have loved to hear William Kelley’s take but I suppose it is indeed simply a case of a small production finding a strong following.

I don’t think I have too much to add to that analysis!

But if I did, I would add that, in addition to the logic of supply and demand, Lamy-Caillat represents a very rare style of white Burgundy that’s not easy to find and thus commands a big premium. That was certainly why I gravitated so strongly to the wines when I first tasted and reviewed them, back when I was working for Decanter.

Last week I drank a few bottles of the 2019 Chassagne-Montrachet, and it showed so brilliantly, I wish every Burgundy lover could taste these wines! Sadly, that is likely not to be.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: How come I don’t see any crazy auction results on CT about these wines? Are the crazy asking prices just phantoms? Like the supposed A-L asking prices that I can’t find evidence of anyone paying?

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.cooked:
Like the supposed A-L asking prices that I can’t find evidence of anyone paying?

Look to the usual single bottle / lot auction sites and you see plenty of A-L lots with bids… Granted, it’s not case quantities

Very very good wines, limited availability and extraordinarily overpriced since the hype. Incredible dry extract and inner sweetness.
And, almost at the same quality level as Vincent Dancer, but not there yet and who’s prices are now beyond nose bleed levels.
There is so much great Chardonnay “out” there in the world but you need to have a strong constitution, the ability to just raise your eyebrows and not be a lemming. Blind tasting is the key.
And I respect William Kelly for what he does so very well but this is Exhibit A. It happened with Coche, then Raveneau, etc etc etc. and now it’s happening with Champagne, “ the new white burgundy.”

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
How come I don’t see any crazy auction results on CT about these wines? Are the crazy asking prices just phantoms? Like the supposed A-L asking prices that I can’t find evidence of anyone paying?

Pricing ex-domaine remains firmly in the two digits, as is the case from importers who allocate the wines to private clients—in the UK, at least.

But, some people are indeed paying those prices, as they are the Arnoux-Lachaux prices, I assure you! And, they appear to be opening the bottles…

posts.name: Nathan Smyth
posts.cooked:
Pricing ex-domaine remains firmly in the two digits

It’s a crime that these Burgundy families don’t get together and form a cooperative, which direct-imports to countries like the USA, and sells directly to retail customers [in countries like the USA].

150 years ago, Richard Wagner was screaming bloody murder about this sort of exploitation of the unsophisticates.

It’s despicable.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Would have loved to hear William Kelley’s take but I suppose it is indeed simply a case of a small production finding a strong following.

I don’t think I have too much to add to that analysis!

But if I did, I would add that, in addition to the logic of supply and demand, Lamy-Caillat represents a very rare style of white Burgundy that’s not easy to find and thus commands a big premium. That was certainly why I gravitated so strongly to the wines when I first tasted and reviewed them, back when I was working for Decanter.

Last week I drank a few bottles of the 2019 Chassagne-Montrachet, and it showed so brilliantly, I wish every Burgundy lover could taste these wines! Sadly, that is likely not to be.

William, I’d be curious as to how you’d describe the style of these wines.

In short: taut but textural, with an incisive, chiseled profile structured by chalky extract.

Sébastian uses a Vaslin mechanical press, and presses long and hard, which extracts from the skins. The wines then spend a year in barrel and a year in tank, picking up texture from the lees, before they are bottled by hand.

link_counts.title: Phenolic grip/tannins in white wine? (Burgundy and others)

posts.name: Mike Kelly
posts.cooked:
Thanks, William.
How much do the styles of Lamy-Caillat and Lamy-Pillot differ? Does Sebastian use the same principles and “targets”?

Thank you,
MK

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thanks, William.
How much do the styles of Lamy-Caillat and Lamy-Pillot differ? Does Sebastian use the same principles and “targets”?

Thank you,
MK

Lamy-Pillot is pressed using a pneumatic press and sees a shorter élevage. They are quite distinct projects!

forgive my ignorance but is this the St. Aubin producer Lamy that does all the Haut Densite cuvees.

No, that is Olivier Lamy of Domaine Hubert Lamy.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Florence Lamy is Olivier’s cousin. Their fathers were brothers, I believe.
She married Sébastian Caillat.
Lamy-Caillat.
Seb has never been to America.
He said that if he ever did come, he would try first and foremost to visit the convenience store where the movie Clerks was filmed, a real life convenience store in Leonardo NJ. He and some high-school buddies were basically obsessed with the movie.
I took his beautiful 6pk wooden boxes there, staged them on the counter like register candy, and took some pictures. I had an hilarious, could-have-been-from-the-movie exchange at the counter with the clerk working that day…
Bright moments, stranger than fiction…
Santé
